w:r.w:t: Muslim Community of the Western Suburbs of Detroit

A single-product e-commerce website for MCWS — the Muslim Community of the Western Suburbs of Detroit. The site sells a branded hoodie produced by a local manufacturer using embroidery and sewing on blank garments. The website represents MCWS only and does not reference the manufacturer or any third-party business.
The site is designed to be polished, minimal, and intentional — black and white with light mint green accents, monochrome logo treatment, and Helvetica typography throughout.
Size only — no color picker needed
Page load transitions, subtle scroll effects
Hero section, product showcase, size selector, add to cart, checkout CTA
MCWS community story, mission, what the merch represents
Post-checkout confirmation with order details and fulfillment info
Protected route; order list with status management

Full-viewport section with GSAP load-in animation. Monochrome MCWS logo, headline, and a single CTA scrolling down to the product. Black background, white type, mint accent on the CTA or a subtle detail.
Large product imagery (multiple angles, embroidery detail). Size selector (S–XL). Price display. Fulfillment method toggle: Shipping vs. Local Pickup. Add to Cart / Buy Now button.
MCWS name, link to About page, and any relevant contact or community links. Minimal.
CTAs, dividers, hover states, subtle highlights
Helvetica is the sole typeface across the entire site — headings, body, UI elements, navigation. Weight variation (light, regular, bold) creates hierarchy. No decorative or secondary fonts.

Page load: staggered fade-in and upward translate on hero elements (logo, headline, CTA). Scroll-triggered: subtle opacity and translate on the product section as it enters viewport. Hover: scale or opacity micro-interactions on buttons and product images. All animations should feel deliberate and restrained — no bouncing, no elastic easing. Use gsap.to() with power2.out or similar.

The MCWS logo (geometric star/mandala + wordmark) will be rendered in monochrome (white on black background) throughout the site. The original gold/teal colors are not used on the website. The full logo with the tagline (“Muslim Community of the Western Suburbs of Detroit”) may appear on the About page; elsewhere, the condensed wordmark or icon alone is sufficient.
Customer selects fulfillment method: Ship to Address or Local Pickup
Customer clicks Buy Now → redirects to Stripe Checkout
Stripe collects payment and shipping address (if shipping selected)
Stripe fires checkout.session.completed webhook to Supabase Edge Function
Edge Function creates order record in Supabase
Edge Function sends SMS/email notification to manufacturer
Customer is redirected to order confirmation page
Stripe Checkout collects shipping address. Manufacturer produces item, generates Shippo label, ships. Tracking number stored on order record. Customer notified with tracking link via email.
No shipping address collected. Order confirmation displays pickup location (mosque address) and instructions. Manufacturer produces item and delivers to mosque for pickup.

On each new order, the manufacturer receives an SMS or email containing: product ordered, size, customer name, fulfillment method (ship or pickup), and shipping address if applicable. This notification is triggered from the Stripe webhook handler. No admin dashboard is required for the manufacturer on day one — notifications are sufficient until order volume justifies a UI.
Stripe address object, null for pickup

Stripe Checkout Sessions handle payment. The site creates a Checkout Session via a Next.js API route (/api/checkout), passing product info, size, quantity, and fulfillment method as metadata. Stripe collects payment info and shipping address (for shipped orders). On completion, Stripe posts to a webhook endpoint (/api/webhooks/stripe) which is a Next.js API route that verifies the signature and writes the order to Supabase.

Phase 1: The manufacturer uses Shippo’s dashboard directly to generate shipping labels for shipped orders. The tracking number is manually entered into the admin panel (or directly in Supabase). Phase 2 (if volume justifies): Integrate Shippo’s API into the admin panel so labels can be generated and tracking auto-populated from within the site.

New order notifications to the manufacturer via SMS (Twilio) or email (Resend / Supabase’s built-in email). SMS is the simpler path given existing Twilio infrastructure from SPRTD Queue, but this is a separate project and should use its own Twilio number or email sender.

A lightweight protected route at /admin/orders. Auth via Supabase (single admin account, email/password). Displays all orders in a table with columns for: date, customer name, size, fulfillment method, payment status, fulfillment status, and tracking number. Each order row is expandable or clickable for full details. Status can be updated manually (pending → producing → shipped/ready_for_pickup → completed). Tracking number field for manual entry (Phase 1). This is intentionally minimal — no analytics, no dashboards, no reports. Just a list of orders and the ability to update their status.

Product images are stored in Supabase Storage (public bucket). Images should be optimized before upload (compressed, appropriate resolution for web). The site uses Next.js Image component for automatic optimization, lazy loading, and responsive sizing. Recommended image set: hero/lifestyle shot (worn or styled), front view, back view, embroidery detail close-up. Images are referenced by URL in the products table (images array field).

NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_URL, NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY, SUPABASE_SERVICE_ROLE_KEY, STRIPE_SECRET_KEY, STRIPE_WEBHOOK_SECRET, NEXT_PUBLIC_STRIPE_PUBLISHABLE_KEY, TWILIO_ACCOUNT_SID / TWILIO_AUTH_TOKEN / TWILIO_PHONE_NUMBER (if SMS notifications)
Stripe Checkout integration with webhook order creation
Shipping and local pickup fulfillment options
SMS/email notification to manufacturer on new orders
Minimal admin panel for order management
GSAP page load and subtle scroll animations
User accounts or order history for customers
Any reference to the manufacturer or third-party business
